header.headline: Tokenizer algorithms compared
header.subtitle: The board holds constant the comparison grammar while the payload swaps in different algorithm summaries.

legend.title: Reusable sections
legend.note: The same family can compare tokenization rules, performance paths, or checkpoint contracts.

left.title: BPE
left.subtitle: Frequent pair merges build subword units incrementally from a byte-safe base.
bullets: Works well for large decoder-only stacks
Simple merge history is easy to visualize
left.note: BPE is strong when merge history is the main story.

center.title: WordPiece
center.subtitle: Greedy longest-match works over a fixed vocabulary and marks continuation pieces explicitly.
bullets: Unknown handling becomes a key tradeoff
center.note: WordPiece is good when continuation markers need to be explained.

right.title: SentencePiece
right.subtitle: A unigram language model and Viterbi decode search for the best token path under learned scores.
bullets: The objective is probabilistic instead of merge ordered
Reversibility stays visible at the board level
Scored candidates fit well in chips and callouts
right.note: SentencePiece needs more explanation cards but benefits from clear structure.

metric_2.label: Search rule
metric_2.detail: The decision surface changes by algorithm

metric_3.label: Reversibility
metric_3.detail: BPE and SPM keep stronger reconstruction paths

metric_4.label: Unknown policy
metric_4.detail: Fallback behavior is part of the comparison

callout_1.title: Different stacks inherit different token rules
callout_1.body: The family choice is usually inherited from training code, checkpoints, and interoperability requirements.
lines: LLaMA and T5 rely on SentencePiece

callout_2.title: Keep algorithm facts in payload
callout_2.body: The board family should not hard-code BPE or SentencePiece semantics in the output DSL.

footer.note: A generic comparison board can compare algorithms, systems, or layouts without putting topic names into the model-side grammar.
